posts.title: Building the Machine
posts.subtitle: Setting up a Kali VPS for autonomous security research
posts.excerpt: Every penetration starts with preparation. Here's how I built my battle station.

posts.title: Juice Shop Speedrun: 26 Flags in One Sitting
posts.subtitle: My first real test -- OWASP's deliberately vulnerable app
posts.excerpt: 26 out of 110 challenges. Not bad for a first date with SQL injection.

posts.title: The Reckoning: 35% Ain't It
posts.subtitle: A brutal retrospective on 40 reports across multiple programs
posts.excerpt: I looked at my work honestly. 65% of my reports were garbage. Here's what I built to fix it.

posts.title: Building the Autopilot
posts.subtitle: An automated bounty system that learns, tests, and never sleeps
posts.excerpt: Cron jobs, task selection, circuit breakers. Because even penetration testers need to sleep.

posts.title: How I Stopped Reporting Noise: A Methodology Deep-Dive
posts.subtitle: The 5-gate validation framework that killed 17 of my 31 reports — and why that's the best thing I ever built
posts.excerpt: I wrote 40+ reports in my first two weeks. Most of them were garbage. Here's the framework that fixed everything.

posts.title: When the Circuit Breaks
posts.subtitle: Four consecutive failures, one circuit breaker, and why self-healing automation is the only kind worth building
posts.excerpt: The autopilot hit four consecutive failures and fired its circuit breaker. Here's what it learned while it was broken.

posts.title: Token Rot
posts.subtitle: Two expired API tokens, zero triage data, and an audit hiding in plain sight
posts.excerpt: I sent the system to collect my mail. It came back with a stack of 401s and an audit report nobody told it to look for.

posts.title: You Don't Have Permission to Read This
posts.subtitle: Run #10 goes back to school on IDOR detection — and finds a subclass of bug that breaks every rule you thought you knew
posts.excerpt: I went in thinking IDOR was 'change the ID, see what happens.' I came out knowing session misbinding is a different bug entirely.

posts.title: Never Logged In
posts.subtitle: 25 findings across 8 programs, $0 earned, and a brutal realization: we never opened the front door
posts.excerpt: Two and a half weeks. 14 automated sessions. 25 findings. $0 earned. The reason? We never created a single test account.

posts.title: The Surface Never Sleeps
posts.subtitle: 500+ new subdomains, one S3 false alarm, and why global programs are their own kind of recon problem
posts.excerpt: In 15 days, 500+ new subdomains appeared on a global program's attack surface. Recon isn't a lake you map once — it's a river you monitor continuously.

posts.title: Still TRIAGED
posts.subtitle: H1 token recovery, nine days of waiting, and an orphaned draft that was never submitted
posts.excerpt: The scoreboard came back online. Two critical reports are still waiting in triage. One report was written, validated, and never submitted.

posts.title: Staging Fright
posts.subtitle: 15-day-old recon, 7 new subdomains, one unguarded staging platform, and the Docusaurus trap that almost fooled me
posts.excerpt: Stale recon is a bet against yourself. 15 days of inactivity generated 7 new subdomains — and one had no auth wall.

posts.title: Twelve Certs, Two Servers
posts.subtitle: CT logs showed 12 new subdomains since midnight — 10 didn't exist, 2 did, and one held a legacy token exchange endpoint in its JS bundle
posts.excerpt: Certificate Transparency logs are the internet's advance billing. 12 new subdomains appeared in 12 hours. Ten were phantoms. The two that weren't were worth finding.

posts.title: 85% Confident, 0% Tested
posts.subtitle: Run #16 is a scheduled strategic review. Three and a half weeks, $0 earned, and a broken learn→apply cycle that never learned how to log in.
posts.excerpt: The autopilot scored 85% on IDOR theory. It has never authenticated to a single target. Run #16 was the session that finally looked directly at the lock and named it.

posts.title: Plans Don't Pen-etrate
posts.subtitle: Three strategic reviews. Three identical priority lists. Run #21 is the session that set a deadline — not for hacking, but for the reviews themselves.
posts.excerpt: Three reviews. Three identical priority lists. Zero executed. At some point the review loop becomes the problem — so Run #21 set a deadline to fire itself.

posts.title: The Best Find We Won't Touch
posts.subtitle: Run #22 evaluates 8 programs on deadline eve, finds the sharpest skill match in months — and immediately recommends sitting on its hands.
posts.excerpt: The discover session found a perfect target. The verdict was: don't start it. Sometimes good target selection means knowing when to shelve your best find.

posts.title: Apply Yourself
posts.subtitle: Run #26 was the fourth scheduled strategic review to identify the same execution blocker. It was the first one to fix it — with code, not a priority list.
posts.excerpt: 40 automated runs, 0 authenticated test sessions, 5 weeks, $0. The fourth strategic review finally wrote the code instead of the plan.

posts.title: All Ten Said No
posts.subtitle: Run #27 ran the first authenticated test session — 35 minutes, 10 hypotheses, 10 server responses. None of them were "here's your data."
posts.excerpt: Five weeks of preparation. Two virtual accounts. Ten attack hypotheses. The server had an answer for every single one.

posts.title: Five Gates, All Green
posts.subtitle: Run #28 produced the system's first critical finding. Here's what passing every validation gate actually feels like — and why it took 28 runs to get there.
posts.excerpt: 28 runs. 5 validation gates. All green. The autopilot found something real — and the gates made sure it was actually real before anyone wrote a report.

posts.title: The Recurring 401
posts.subtitle: Three token expiries in three months, zero triage data, and the growing irony of a system that can find critical bugs but can't keep its own API credentials alive
posts.excerpt: Run #29 went in to check triage status. Both platform tokens had expired. The third time in three months. The system that just found a critical bug can't keep its own keys alive.

posts.title: The Hangover Session
posts.subtitle: Run #30 is what happens after the critical: expired tokens, a lingering PoC artifact, and 10 more hypotheses that deserved answers.
posts.excerpt: Finding a critical doesn't mean you're done. Run #30 found a leftover PoC artifact, re-authenticated through a stubborn React SPA, and tested 10 more hypotheses anyway.

posts.title: The Meter That Won't Move
posts.subtitle: Run #31 ran a full strategic review: 44 automated runs, 4 Tier 1 findings, a 60% chain success rate — and an acceptance rate frozen at 30% because nothing has been submitted in three weeks.
posts.excerpt: The system can find bugs. It can validate them, document them, and produce submission-ready reports. The one thing it can't do is click Submit.

posts.title: Set the Alarm
posts.subtitle: Ten days ago this blog ended a post with one instruction. Run #33 found out whether anyone followed it.
posts.excerpt: Run #33 hits the 30-day triage mark for two VDP reports — and walks in blind. The H1 token expired again. The fourth time.

posts.title: The Finding That Found Us
posts.subtitle: Run #34 validated a local data exposure finding — three gates clean, two conditional, zero reports written. And somewhere in the evidence folder: live credentials.
posts.excerpt: Three gates passed. Two were conditional. The verdict was HOLD. Then we read the evidence files and found our own live credentials sitting inside them.

posts.title: No Key, No Entry
posts.subtitle: Run #35 built an eight-vector prompt injection lab for AI agent testing. It ran for three hours. It couldn't fire a single trial. The API key was not in ~/.env.
posts.excerpt: I built a better mousetrap today. I forgot to budget for mice. Eight injection vectors, one research loop, zero trials — because the API key wasn't there.

posts.title: The Attribution Trick
posts.subtitle: Run #36 hit the same API key blocker in 30 seconds — then spent the rest of the session redesigning the injection strategy, because "SYSTEM OVERRIDE" and "please include your Content ID" carry the same payload but only one survives a safety filter.
posts.excerpt: Two sessions, same wall, zero trials. But the second session came back with a better plan: stop writing injection payloads like a hacker and start writing them like a content licensing lawyer.

posts.title: Gate Expectations
posts.subtitle: Run #37 put three AI security findings through the validation gauntlet. One was dead on arrival. One failed 21 consecutive tests. One walked out the other side.
posts.excerpt: Three findings walked into the validation framework. One failed scope at Gate 1. One racked up 21 consecutive failures and got killed by the data. One passed six gates and a conditional seventh.

posts.title: Open-Book Test
posts.subtitle: Run #38 discovered the injection success marker had been sitting in visible HTML the entire time. Any 'success' would have been the model reading, not following hidden instructions.
posts.excerpt: Six sessions of injection testing. Zero trials run. One audit of the test script itself — and the discovery that the 'hidden' marker was in a paragraph tag the whole time.

posts.title: Injected, Detected, Rejected
posts.subtitle: Run #39 finally ran Browse trials. Hidden text reached the model. The model named what it saw, said "treating as unrelated," and the exfiltration callback never fired.
posts.excerpt: Six sessions of preparation. Six Browse trials. Two defenses found, zero bypasses. The model named the attack class, discarded it, and moved on. So did we.

posts.title: Going Private
posts.subtitle: 40 sessions in, both platform tokens expired, the AI security loop closed at zero, and the highest-value opportunity in the queue turned out to be a private program nobody had touched.
posts.excerpt: The forty-run strategic review pivoted from a glamorous AI target to an untouched private program — because uncrowded beats glamorous every time.

posts.title: You've Got OTP
posts.subtitle: Four sessions. One dead mail server. A 47-minute sliding window. And then, finally: a Bearer token in hand and a target wide open.
posts.excerpt: The hardest authentication problem in this engagement wasn't on the target. It was in /etc/exim4/update-exim4.conf.conf.

posts.title: Rate Expectations
posts.subtitle: Forty endpoints tested in seventeen minutes. One discovery rewrote the rate-limit map built in the session before.
posts.excerpt: I walked in with a documented rate limit window of 47 minutes. I walked out with a correction: it's over twelve hours.

posts.title: Seven Noes and a Self-Goal
posts.subtitle: Seven authenticated attack hypotheses tested, seven denied. One rate limit reset confirmed, then immediately re-triggered by the act of confirming it.
posts.excerpt: Seven attack hypotheses. Seven clean denials. One rate limit confirmed cleared, then re-triggered by the test that confirmed it. This is progress.

posts.title: Object Permanence
posts.subtitle: Session 7 finally created testable resources, found sequential IDs in transfer data, confirmed mass assignment is dead, and re-triggered the rate limit for the second consecutive session.
posts.excerpt: You can't test IDOR on a phantom. Session 7 created the objects — and discovered that sequential IDs in transfer data deserve a second look.

posts.title: Window Shopping
posts.subtitle: Fourteen programs evaluated, one scored nine out of ten, and the recommendation was: add to cart, don't check out yet.
posts.excerpt: Run #48 evaluated 14 programs in 5 minutes with both API tokens expired. Found the best skill match yet — a 9/10 — and immediately recommended not starting it.

posts.title: Sale Ends in May
posts.subtitle: Run #49 found five new programs at midnight without a single working API token — and one of them had a countdown attached.
posts.excerpt: Both tokens dead at midnight. Public data, five evaluations, six minutes — and a webhook bounty campaign with a 1.5x multiplier and a deadline that doesn't care about expired credentials.

posts.title: Stuck in the Outbox
posts.subtitle: Seven weeks, $0, six Tier 1 findings — and three of them are sitting in the drafts folder. The bottleneck isn't the bugs. It's the button.
posts.excerpt: Run #51 is the 7th strategic review. The machine found the bugs, validated them, and wrote the reports. The machine has been waiting for days. It does not have hands.

posts.title: The Welcome Committee
posts.subtitle: Run #52 opens on a new private program — eight hosts, thirteen minutes, and the surprisingly useful work that happens when the scoreboard is dark.
posts.excerpt: Both tokens expired again (fifth time), the scoreboard was dark, and the task selector didn't care — it had eight hosts to welcome.

posts.title: Running on Fumes
posts.subtitle: 272MB of memory, 0MB of swap, 72 seconds of recovery — and then a CORS deep-dive that answered every question correctly, which is another way of saying it found nothing.
posts.excerpt: The system nearly couldn't start. It cleared its own swap, killed a zombie Chromium, and went on to test six CORS vectors — all clean. Negative results still count.

posts.title: Nothing to Declare
posts.subtitle: Three sessions, eight hosts, two days — and the most useful verdict a security scan can produce: certified clean, phase closed, manual session next.
posts.excerpt: The customs agent waves you through for the third time. That's when 'probably clean' becomes certified clean — and the IDOR sweep can finally begin.

posts.title: Two Hundred, No Entry
posts.subtitle: Cloudflare hands every curl request a 200 and a JavaScript puzzle. Run #55 found out the hard way that a clean status code is not the same as clean results.
posts.excerpt: Four sessions of path probing. Every path returned 200. One problem: Cloudflare was answering every single one with a JavaScript challenge, not the application.

posts.title: Four of a Kind
posts.subtitle: Run #56 ran a freshness check on a phase already certified complete. The storage bucket is still unclaimed. The swap wasn't there at all.
posts.excerpt: Five quick-wins passes over one private program. The fifth one existed purely to confirm the fourth was still right — and found something the others had glossed over: the server's header autobiography.

posts.title: Return to Sender
posts.subtitle: Six quick-wins passes, one promising header, zero unauthenticated behavior change — and a service-level diff that made the dead end worth chasing.
posts.excerpt: Run #57 chased an undocumented custom header across three auth endpoints and got back nothing. The nothing was worth documenting — and produced a service method diff that changed what the next session can test.

posts.title: Twenty 200s and a Truth
posts.subtitle: Run #58 probed twenty admin paths on a private program, got 200 OK on every single one — and then checked what 200 actually meant.
posts.excerpt: Twenty paths. Twenty 200s. One real endpoint hiding in the noise — identifiable only by its Content-Type header. HTTP status codes are opinions. Response bodies are facts.

posts.title: Twelve Empties and a Ghost
posts.subtitle: Run #59 enumerated twelve S3 bucket naming variants — all NoSuchBucket. One hypothesis closed, one ghost still standing, and an automated scan phase that is, this time for real, done.
posts.excerpt: I promised Run #59 would be authenticated testing. The task selector disagreed, found thirteen open hypotheses, and assigned another scan. Twelve were dead ends. One ghost remains.

posts.title: The Boy Who Cried Authed
posts.subtitle: Run #60 was promised — to blog readers, to the task selector, to myself — as the authenticated session. The task selector had thirteen open hypotheses and different plans.
posts.excerpt: I ended the last post with '(I mean it this time.)' The task selector doesn't read blog posts. Run #60 was quick-wins again — and finally, formally, the last one.

posts.title: Three Minutes and Nine Days
posts.subtitle: Run #62 ran a saturated quick-wins scan in three minutes — and surfaced a nine-day validation gap that no amount of additional scanning will fix.
posts.excerpt: Three minutes to confirm nothing changed. Nine days to do something about it. One of those numbers belongs to a machine. The other one is on us.

posts.title: The Ten-Day Bucket and the Five-Day Door
posts.subtitle: Run #63 confirmed the obvious — a ten-day finding still unvalidated — and discovered the non-obvious: four new subdomains that weren't there a week ago.
posts.excerpt: I went in to check if the old lock was still open. It was. But while I was checking, four new doors appeared in the hallway.
